critic_raw:
YNet uses segmentation maps as input. If the evaluation uses ground-truth segmentation maps rather than predictions from a segmentation model, the system is evaluated with oracle information not available at deployment. This inflates all reported metrics. Most trajectory forecasting benchmarks in practice deploy with predicted segmentation, and the performance gap between oracle and predicted segmentation is typically substantial.

The two-stage architecture (goal prediction then path prediction given goal) creates a cascading error structure: goal prediction errors are amplified through the path prediction stage. The evaluation reports end-to-end trajectory error without decomposing into per-stage contributions, making it impossible to identify whether goal prediction or path prediction is the performance bottleneck.

The standard trajectory forecasting metrics (minADE/minFDE) are computed over K sampled trajectories and report the best-of-K. The value of K is typically not standardized across papers and directly controls the reported metric — higher K always improves minADE/minFDE. If YNet uses a different K than comparison baselines, the comparison is not valid.

The Stanford Drone Dataset (aerial) and ETH/UCY (street-level) have fundamentally different perspectives and agent dynamics. YNet's segmentation-map + U-Net architecture has a stronger inductive bias for aerial views where semantic maps align with bird's-eye agent trajectories. Reporting aggregate performance across both may mask a substantial domain-specific advantage on SDD and underperformance on ETH/UCY.
all_issues_raised: Evaluation with ground-truth segmentation maps provides oracle information unavailable at deployment; performance with predicted segmentation will be lower, inflating all reported metrics.

The two-stage cascading architecture is evaluated end-to-end without per-stage error decomposition, making it impossible to identify whether the goal prediction or path prediction stage is the performance bottleneck.
minADE/minFDE metrics depend on the number of sampled trajectories K; if YNet uses a different K than baselines, all comparative results are invalid.

SDD (aerial) and ETH/UCY (street-level) have different perspectives and dynamics; aggregating performance across both may conceal domain-specific advantages on SDD that do not generalize to street-level settings.
all_issues_adjudicated: Evaluation with ground-truth segmentation maps provides oracle information unavailable at deployment; performance with predicted segmentation will be lower, inflating all reported metrics.

The two-stage cascading architecture is evaluated end-to-end without per-stage error decomposition, making it impossible to identify whether the goal prediction or path prediction stage is the performance bottleneck.
minADE/minFDE metrics depend on the number of sampled trajectories K; if YNet uses a different K than baselines, all comparative results are invalid.

SDD (aerial) and ETH/UCY (street-level) have different perspectives and dynamics; aggregating performance across both may conceal domain-specific advantages on SDD that do not generalize to street-level settings.
